messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Internal model components correlate with abilities but
correlation is not causation; how can we identify which
components causally implement a given capability and use that
for editing or interpretation?


### Proposed method
Define a component family and an interventional knob. Choose a granular basis to probe (e.g., SAE features, MLP neurons, attention heads, or low-rank subspaces). For each component c, implement a parametric intervention I(c; α) that scales, nulls, or injects activation with a signed strength α. Calibrate α per component to achieve a fixed target KL divergence δ between the model’s clean and intervened token distributions, so all components are compared at equal perturbation strength. For any capability of interest, specify a measurable scorer (unit tests or a judge LLM) and a token-level Component Dependency Measure that compares p(y_t | history, I off) and p(y_t | history, I on) under the calibrated δ; aggregate per-token effects into capability-specific scores.

Actively harvest diagnostic contexts that maximize causal signal. Starting from seed prompts that elicit the capability, run a search loop that proposes context mutations (paraphrases, harder variants, distractors) and selects those where the calibrated intervention on any candidate component yields large, reliable output divergences or capability score deltas. Interleave model runs with selection to concentrate the diagnostic set on regions where causal effects are identifiable, not merely correlated. This planner-style data collection increases power and reduces confounding by ensuring comparisons are made where the capability is actually exercised.

Estimate causal responsibility with knockout–rescue tests and robust aggregation. For each component, compute calibrated knockout effects on the diagnostic set and confirm directionality via anti-interventions (amplify vs suppress). Perform rescue by injecting a downstream surrogate signal to test whether the observed impairment is specifically mediated by the targeted pathway. Quantify redundancy and synergy via pairwise and small-set ablations; fit a minimal hitting set of components whose joint knockout flips the capability metric, and estimate marginal contributions with greedy Shapley-style approximations. Aggregate effect sizes across contexts with heavy-tailed estimators (e.g., Student’s t regression or median-of-means) and report confidence intervals via bootstrap, yielding a ranked list of causally responsible components and minimal causal cores.

Edit within a trust region. Attach light-weight gates to the identified components (scalars or tiny MLPs) and optimize them to attenuate or enhance the capability on the diagnostic set while enforcing a per-batch KL budget to the original model, preserving global behavior. Use a trust-region update that maximizes the desired capability objective (or minimizes an undesired one) under the KL constraint; stop early when the capability change saturates or the KL budget is met. Validate on held-out contexts, and, if needed, widen edits by adding or removing components from the causal core based on post-edit re-scoring.

Produce output-grounded interpretations. For each causally identified component, automatically summarize its function by collecting tokens and behaviors whose probabilities most increase or decrease under calibrated interventions on the diagnostic set, clustering them, and prompting a judge model to produce concise hypotheses. Track the component dependency curves over generation steps to reveal when and how the capability is mediated. Package each component with: its effect size and CI, minimal counterfactual examples demonstrating knockout–rescue, the learned gate value if edited, and a natural-language description tied to its downstream impacts.

### Rationale
Causality requires interventions and counterfactuals, not correlations. By calibrating per-component interventions to a fixed KL distance, we obtain fair, comparable effect sizes across heterogeneous units and avoid over-penalizing sensitive layers. The active context search focuses measurement on cases where the capability is engaged, increasing statistical power and reducing spurious associations. Knockout–rescue tests and small-set ablations distinguish direct mediators from passengers and reveal redundancy, while robust aggregation prevents a few outlier prompts from dominating conclusions.

For editing, KL-constrained, trust-region gating provides local, controllable changes to the identified causal pathways while guarding against off-target drift. Output-based summaries convert raw effect measurements into human-usable interpretations, grounding component descriptions in their demonstrable downstream consequences rather than input correlations. Together, these steps reliably identify which components causally implement a capability and turn that knowledge into targeted, safe edits and clear interpretations.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [LLM_NLP] Automatically Interpreting Millions of Features in Large Language Models (2410.13928)
   problem: Some SAE features are better characterized by their causal effects on a model’s outputs rather than by correlational input contexts, which existing methods can miss; practitioners also need steerable, causally grounded interpretations.
   method:  They propose intervention scoring, which quantifies interpretability of a feature by the decrease in a scorer LLM’s surprisal about an interpretation when conditioned on generations from an intervened model. Intervention strength is calibrated per-feature via a target KL-divergence between clean and intervened logits, enabling fair comparison at fixed strengths. They automatically generate ‘output’ interpretations by showing the explainer only the tokens whose logits increase most after intervention, and validate that SAE features score higher than random SAEs or shuffled interpretations. Results show later layers have more interpretable output effects and that features with low correlational scores can be well explained through their downstream impacts.

2. [ROBOTICS] Search-Based Task Planning with Learned Skill Effect Models for Lifelong Robotic Manipulation (2109.08771)
   problem: Learning accurate, planning-relevant models of skill effects is hard because the distribution of states visited during planning/execution is unknown a priori, and the robot must continually incorporate new skills in a lifelong setting.
   method:  Interleave SEM training with planner-driven data collection. Initialize each new skill’s SEM with single-skill executions sampled from the known initial state distribution, then run the planner with current SEMs on training tasks to generate candidate paths. Sample paths from the planning graph—biased toward longer paths and those that include newly added skills—simulate them to collect (state, parameter, terminal state, cost) transitions, filter duplicates, and fine-tune SEMs for both new and existing skills as needed. This yields data concentrated on planning-relevant regions and supports incremental addition of skills; empirically, training on planner-generated data achieves higher success with fewer samples than training on random executions.

3. [ROBOTICS] Visual Geometric Skill Inference by Watching Human Demonstration (1911.04418)
   problem: Multiple associations can define the same skill and some may be occluded or leave the FOV, so the model must robustly and consistently select valid associations from only one demonstration.
   method:  Optimize each kernel with Incremental Maximum Entropy IRL over the demonstration frames. Define a reward r_t based on the decrease in overall control error between successive frames and assume a variational expert with confidence α (truncated-normal/Boltzmann selection over actions). Maximize demonstration likelihood by estimating the partition function via Monte Carlo and backpropagating through g_k and the GNN to update kernel parameters. Add a residual sum of weights (RSW) regularizer to make the select-out sparse/deterministic over the top-p alternatives while preserving the ability to switch to viable candidates when others are unobservable, yielding robust association inference.

4. [MULTIMODAL] VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation (1708.04686)
   problem: Existing VQA systems lack explicit supervision that links questions/answers to the precise image regions that constitute visual evidence, leading to weak or misaligned attentions; related annotations like human attention maps are coarse and bounding boxes often do not directly answer diverse question types.
   method:  Introduce VQS, a dataset that explicitly links COCO instance segmentations to VQA question–answer pairs by asking annotators to select the segmentation(s) that visually answer each question (with tight boxes when needed). This produces 37,868 images, 96,508 questions, 108,537 segmentations, and 43,725 boxes, enabling transfer of supervision between segmentation and VQA and supporting both supervised attention and new grounded tasks.

5. [MULTIMODAL] Multi-Modal Hallucination Control by Visual Information Grounding (2403.14003)
   problem: Autoregressive vision-language models (VLMs) often hallucinate objects or attributes because the model’s reliance on the visual prompt fades as more tokens are generated, and there is no principled, annotation-free way to quantify this grounding loss.
   method:  Introduce a visual Prompt Dependency Measure (PDM) that compares the conditioned distribution p(y_t | y_<t, x, c) to the unconditioned language-only distribution p(y_t | y_<t, x) using distances such as the Hellinger metric (PDM-H). This quantifies how much a token depends on the image versus the language prior. Empirically, PDM decreases with token position (conditioning dilution/fading memory), and lower PDM correlates with increased hallucinated objects in captions, while also identifying ‘contextual pressure’ cases (e.g., function words) where low PDM does not imply hallucination.

6. [MULTIMODAL] Words or Vision: Do Vision-Language Models Have Blind Faith in Text? (2503.02199)
   problem: Current VLM evaluations largely assume text inputs are consistent with images, leaving it unclear how models behave when visual and textual information conflict or when text is noisy, misleading, or irrelevant.
   method:  Construct a cross-task benchmark that injects three controlled textual variations—Match, Corruption, and Irrelevance—into four vision-centric datasets (VQAv2, DocVQA, MathVista, and a real-world Brand Recognition task). Define the Text Preference Ratio (TPR) to quantify whether models choose text- vs image-based answers when they disagree, alongside normalized accuracy to measure robustness. Evaluating 10 VLMs reveals a pervasive 'blind faith in text' (high TPR and large accuracy drops under corrupted text), especially in open models, highlighting safety risks in realistic settings such as brand recognition on webpages.

7. [CV] Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion (2012.02107)
   problem: Per-object, per-pixel feed-forward segmentations often conflict in overlap regions (multiple objects claiming the same pixels), especially under heavy or same-category occlusions, leading to erroneous masks and classifications.
   method:  Introduce an Occlusion Reasoning Module (ORM) that operates on the per-object likelihood maps. It detects conflicts where two objects both predict foreground, resolves them via pixel-level competition by assigning each contested pixel to the object with higher foreground likelihood, and then recovers the pairwise occlusion order by comparing the number of reassigned pixels in the conflict set. Using this order, it enforces consistent labeling in overlaps and feeds the corrected occlusion priors back into the CompositionalNet to mask occluded features and recompute class likelihoods, iterating this top-down self-correction to improve both segmentation and classification under occlusion.

8. [CV] Real-time Hyperspectral Imaging in Hardware via Trained Metasurface Encoders (2204.02084)
   problem: Fixed filter banks cannot optimally capture task-specific spectral features for downstream vision tasks like semantic segmentation, especially when RGB cues are ambiguous (e.g., real vs. artificial fruits with similar colors).
   method:  Develop a differentiable, physics-aware encoder–decoder framework (d-ALFRED) that jointly optimizes metasurface projector responses and geometry with the downstream decoder via backpropagation. The encoder is modeled by coupled-mode theory (with learnable resonance parameters mapped from geometry), enabling gradient flow through the metasurface transfer function; a U-Net decoder is trained end-to-end for pixel-wise classification. The learned spectral encoders yield compact barcodes that preserve discriminative cues, achieving mIoU ~0.81 (simulation) and ~0.74 (fabricated devices), outperforming an RGB-only baseline (~0.68 mIoU) and significantly improving IoU/F1 on challenging real–artificial fruit pairs.

9. [CV] PrPSeg: Universal Proposition Learning for Panoramic Renal Pathology Segmentation (2402.19286)
   problem: With partially labeled images, it is hard to enforce spatial correlations between labeled and unlabeled classes during training, limiting comprehensive multi-class segmentation.
   method:  Define an anatomy loss that operationalizes the universal proposition matrix for online semi-supervised learning: for a labeled class i, predictions for another class j on the same image are constrained so that (i) if i ⊃ j, j’s prediction stays within i; (ii) if i ⊂ j, j’s prediction covers i; and (iii) if i and j are exclusive, their overlap is minimized. This anatomy loss is combined with supervised BCE and Dice losses (weighted by λupl) to inject inter-object relational constraints and improve comprehensive segmentation.

10. [RL] Conformal Constrained Policy Optimization for Cost-Effective LLM Agents (2511.11828)
   problem: Directly learning set-valued (conformal) policies is combinatorial, and the distribution shift between behavior and target policies complicates reliable coverage calibration in sequential settings.
   method:  Introduce CCPO, which parameterizes a score policy π(a|o) and a threshold κ, and defines a stochastic surrogate S_{π,κ} that places uniform probability over the conformal action set C_{π,κ}(o). Train π with an actor–critic using V-trace off-policy corrections from π to S_{π,κ} with clipped importance weights, and perform a trust-region CPO update that optimizes advantages under a KL constraint while enforcing an upper-bounded coverage constraint; replace the non-differentiable indicator in C with a sigmoid softmask for stable gradients. Update κ online via decaying-step-size conformal prediction to track the target 1−α coverage, followed by a final batch calibration step; under mild assumptions this yields asymptotic 1−α coverage (and O(T^{-1/2+ξ}) coverage regret in adversarial streams) while minimizing cost.

11. [RL] Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL (2406.01939)
   problem: In supply chain RL (and many long-horizon MDPs), simulating a single trajectory under a state-dependent policy is a bottleneck because policy evaluations are inherently serial and expensive (often DNN-based), while standard parallel discrete event methods like Time Warp suffer rollbacks and GPU-unfriendly message passing.
   method:  Introduce Picard iteration for policy simulation: split the horizon [T] across M (virtual) processes and initialize a cache of actions for all time-steps. In each iteration, every process sweeps the entire trajectory but evaluates the policy π only on its assigned time-steps, using cached actions elsewhere (or aϕ if infeasible), while applying the cheap system transition f at every step; at iteration end, each process writes its evaluated actions back to the cache. This enables batched, GPU-friendly evaluation of π with no message passing and provably converges to the sequential trajectory in at most T iterations (Algorithm 1; correctness Proposition 2.1; batching and speedup discussion).

12. [MULTIMODAL] BabyWalk: Going Farther in Vision-and-Language Navigation by Taking Baby Steps (2005.04625)
   problem: Direct reinforcement learning on long-horizon VLN instructions is unstable, and imitation learning on whole trajectories does not adequately prepare agents for long, uncertain executions.
   method:  Use a two-phase training pipeline: (1) micro-level imitation learning where each BABY-STEP is learned independently with the agent 'preset' in expert context to match expert sub-trajectories; (2) curriculum-based reinforcement learning that progressively increases the number of consecutive BABY-STEPs the agent must execute (lectures), providing expert history up to the start and optimizing a fidelity-oriented reward (CLS) via policy gradients. This curriculum (up to 4 lectures) consistently improves over direct RL on full instructions, with stepwise gains in SDTW and CLS.

13. [IR_REC] FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding (2406.15657)
   problem: Inference-time relevance feedback for retrievers typically relies on cross-encoder scores and KL divergence, but listwise LLM rerankers output only orderings, making standard distillation inapplicable and potentially missing stronger supervision signals.
   method:  Use the LLM reranker’s ranked ordering as the supervision signal for updating retriever query embeddings, optimizing with the weighted RankNet loss over retrieved candidates to convert orderings into a differentiable objective. This enables relevance feedback without numeric scores and leverages the stronger listwise signal. On BEIR, LLM-based feedback improves recall@100 over cross-encoder feedback (e.g., 71.2% vs 69.0% average), and combining both signals yields further gains (72.0%).

14. [IR_REC] Intervention Harvesting for Context-Dependent Examination-Bias Estimation (1811.01802)
   problem: Unbiased learning-to-rank from clicks requires accurate examination-bias (propensity) estimates, but existing methods are restricted to the Position-Based Model (PBM) that ignores query/user context and often rely on intrusive randomized swaps or difficult document-level relevance modeling.
   method:  Introduce the Contextual Position-Based Model (CPBM), which models examination as Pr(E=1 | k, x) depending on rank k and context x, and learn it via a neural network h(k, x). Develop an AllPairs estimator that harvests implicit interventions from logs of at least two historic rankers (e.g., A/B tests or stationary deployments): define interventional sets S_{k,k'} of (x, d) that appear at both positions under different rankers, compute assignment weights q_k(x, d), form IPS-weighted click labels, and jointly fit h and an average-relevance model g(k, k', x) by maximizing a weighted cross-entropy objective. This controls for unobserved relevance without explicit swaps and yields context-specific propensities.

15. [IR_REC] Hybrid Inverted Index Is a Robust Accelerator for Dense Retrieval (2210.05521)
   problem: Selecting salient query terms can add undesirable online cost if it relies on heavy neural processing, yet accurate lexical cues are needed to recover relevant documents missed by clustering.
   method:  HI2 makes query-time term selection lightweight by performing all heavy term scoring offline on documents (BM25 or BERT+MLP), storing per-term scores and the global average score per term. At search time, it selects all tokens for short queries and only the top-K_T2 tokens by average score for long queries, avoiding neural computation on queries. This keeps query dispatch overhead negligible while preserving the lexical benefits that improve candidate recall and overall efficiency.

16. [SPEECH] DNCASR: End-to-End Training for Speaker-Attributed ASR (2506.01916)
   problem: Speaker-attributed ASR for long multi-party meetings needs consistent meeting-wide speaker indices and accurate attribution in overlapping speech, but prior integrated or parallel systems either require non-neural clustering or suffer from misalignment between ASR outputs and speaker index prediction.
   method:  Propose DNCASR, an end-to-end trainable architecture with two encoders (a waveform encoder for local token-level acoustic/lexical features and a speaker encoder for window-level speaker embeddings) and two linked decoders (an SOT ASR decoder and a neural clustering (DNC) decoder). A novel cross-attention link allows the DNC decoder to attend directly to the ASR decoder’s token-level hidden features (W_CA), aligning predicted speaker indices with the serialized word tokens and enabling joint optimization under a single loss. Compared to a parallel DNC+ASR baseline, DNCASR yields lower speaker-attributed error (e.g., AMI-MDM Eval cpWER 31.5% with CDA vs 34.6% parallel; 9.0% relative reduction) and larger gains on multi-talker segments.

17. [SPEECH] Unlocking Speech Instruction Data Potential with Query Rewriting (2507.08603)
   problem: Validating that TTS-synthesized speech preserves the linguistic content of the source text is unreliable with a single ASR or WER-only checks; ASR errors lead to improper filtering and dataset bias.
   method:  Use multi-agent annotation and verification: transcribe each synthesized audio with three diverse ASR models (whisper-large-v3, canary-1b, parakeet-tdt-1.1b) and compute semantic similarity to the original text using three embedding models (gte-large-en-v1.5, mxbai-embed-large-v1, stella_en_400M_v5). Average across ASRs and embeddings to form a SIM score and filter with a threshold (α=0.9). This ensemble reduces annotation errors and aligns well with WER (e.g., >98% consistency when WER=0) and improves WER over single-ASR selection (Table 4).

18. [SPEECH] RED-ACE: Robust Error Detection for ASR using Confidence Embeddings (2203.07172)
   problem: ASR error detection models typically rely only on the transcription text and ignore auxiliary ASR signals like word-level confidences, limiting their ability to accurately flag recognition errors.
   method:  Introduce RED-ACE, a BERT-based sequence tagger augmented with a confidence embedding layer. ASR word-level confidence scores are quantized into bins and mapped via a learned embedding matrix; the confidence embedding is summed with the token, segment, and position embeddings to form each token’s input. This joint, contextualized encoding of text and confidence yields statistically significant F1 gains over text-only BERT and over naive score fusion (e.g., appending raw scores, BERT C) on LibriSpeech clean/other (e.g., +4.9% and +7.4% F1), remains robust under cross-pool shifts (+6.3% and +9.9% F1), and generalizes across different ASR models (+2.6% and +3.8% F1).

19. [ROBOTICS] Statler: State-Maintaining Language Models for Embodied Reasoning (2306.17840)
   problem: LLM-based robot planners (e.g., Code-as-Policies) condition only on action/observation histories and struggle with long-horizon temporal reasoning and latent/unobservable dynamics, leading to brittle behavior on multi-step tasks.
   method:  Statler introduces explicit world-state maintenance with two prompted LLMs. A world-state reader takes the user query plus the current JSON-formatted state and generates executable code for the robot; when state changes are implicated, the code calls update_wm to invoke a world-state writer. The writer updates the state estimate based on the action/query, and the updated state is stored in external memory and conditions future decisions. This model-based extension of CaP simplifies tracking and consulting state at each step and yields large gains on tabletop manipulation tasks and a real UR5 setup, especially on temporal queries (e.g., 83% vs. 31% on Pick-and-Place temporal queries; higher episode success across domains).

20. [ROBOTICS] Kinematic-aware Prompting for Generalizable Articulated Object Manipulation with LLMs (2311.02847)
   problem: LLM-based robotic systems struggle to manipulate diverse articulated objects because they lack an explicit, uniform understanding of object kinematics (parts, joints, and contact points), especially in real-world settings.
   method:  Introduce a Unified Kinematic Knowledge Parser that converts each object's geometric-linked parts, joint types/properties, and contact locations into a structured textual (.xml-like) description that LLMs can reliably parse. In simulation this info is extracted directly; in the real world it is obtained via a perception pipeline (Grounding-DINO + Segment Anything for segmentation and GAPartNet for actionable parts and joint estimation). This unified, code-like description supplies LLMs with the precise kinematic context needed for manipulation policy generation.

21. [ROBOTICS] Robust Instant Policy: Leveraging Student's t-Regression Model for Robust In-context Imitation Learning of Robot Manipulation (2506.15157)
   problem: LLM-based in-context imitation learning for robot manipulation can hallucinate, producing outlier trajectories that deviate from few-shot demonstrations, leading to failures in continuous control.
   method:  Robust Instant Policy (RIP) queries an LLM instant policy (via KAT) multiple times on the same context to sample Q candidate trajectories, then fits a Student's t-regression model Sθ(a_t|t;ν) with neural networks for mean and variance to the sampled actions over time, leveraging heavy-tailed robustness to outliers. The final plan is the time-wise mean of the fitted t-distribution, effectively ignoring hallucinated trajectories. Ablations show ν=1.5 outperforms Gaussian (ν→∞), and RIP improves success rates by 26% over KAT and 16% over a Gaussian aggregator across 10 manipulation tasks, especially in low-data regimes.

## Tie-breaking
When between two adjacent scores, default to the LOWER. Resist
optimism bias toward "novel". Score against the literature
signal only; do not apply an additional penalty when the method
reuses inspired seeds (the seeds are context, not a benchmark).

Round 1/10.

1. Weight Patching: Toward Source-Level Mechanistic Localization in LLMs ( 2026, cites: 0)
   Mechanistic interpretability seeks to localize model behavior to the internal components that causally realize it. Prior work has advanced activation-space localization and causal tracing, but modules that appear important in activation space may merely aggregate or amplify upstream signals rather than encode the target capability in their own parameters. To address this gap, we propose Weight Patching, a parameter-space intervention method for source-oriented analysis in paired same-architecture models that differ in how strongly they express a target capability under the inputs of interest. Given a base model and a behavior-specialized counterpart, Weight Patching replaces selected module weights from the specialized model into the base model under a fixed input. We instantiate the method on instruction following and introduce a framework centered on a vector-anchor behavioral interface that provides a shared internal criterion for whether a task-relevant control state has been formed or recovered in open-ended generation. Under this framework, the analysis reveals a hierarchy from shallow candidate source-side carriers to aggregation and routing modules, and further to downstream execution circuits. The recovered component scores can also guide mechanism-aware model merging, improving selective fusion across the evaluated expert combinations and providing additional external validation.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Internal model components correlate with abilities but
correlation is not causation; how can we identify which
components causally implement a given capability and use that
for editing or interpretation?


### Proposed method
Define a component family and an interventional knob. Choose a granular basis to probe (e.g., SAE features, MLP neurons, attention heads, or low-rank subspaces). For each component c, implement a parametric intervention I(c; α) that scales, nulls, or injects activation with a signed strength α. Calibrate α per component to achieve a fixed target KL divergence δ between the model’s clean and intervened token distributions, so all components are compared at equal perturbation strength. For any capability of interest, specify a measurable scorer (unit tests or a judge LLM) and a token-level Component Dependency Measure that compares p(y_t | history, I off) and p(y_t | history, I on) under the calibrated δ; aggregate per-token effects into capability-specific scores.

Actively harvest diagnostic contexts that maximize causal signal. Starting from seed prompts that elicit the capability, run a search loop that proposes context mutations (paraphrases, harder variants, distractors) and selects those where the calibrated intervention on any candidate component yields large, reliable output divergences or capability score deltas. Interleave model runs with selection to concentrate the diagnostic set on regions where causal effects are identifiable, not merely correlated. This planner-style data collection increases power and reduces confounding by ensuring comparisons are made where the capability is actually exercised.

Estimate causal responsibility with knockout–rescue tests and robust aggregation. For each component, compute calibrated knockout effects on the diagnostic set and confirm directionality via anti-interventions (amplify vs suppress). Perform rescue by injecting a downstream surrogate signal to test whether the observed impairment is specifically mediated by the targeted pathway. Quantify redundancy and synergy via pairwise and small-set ablations; fit a minimal hitting set of components whose joint knockout flips the capability metric, and estimate marginal contributions with greedy Shapley-style approximations. Aggregate effect sizes across contexts with heavy-tailed estimators (e.g., Student’s t regression or median-of-means) and report confidence intervals via bootstrap, yielding a ranked list of causally responsible components and minimal causal cores.

Edit within a trust region. Attach light-weight gates to the identified components (scalars or tiny MLPs) and optimize them to attenuate or enhance the capability on the diagnostic set while enforcing a per-batch KL budget to the original model, preserving global behavior. Use a trust-region update that maximizes the desired capability objective (or minimizes an undesired one) under the KL constraint; stop early when the capability change saturates or the KL budget is met. Validate on held-out contexts, and, if needed, widen edits by adding or removing components from the causal core based on post-edit re-scoring.

Produce output-grounded interpretations. For each causally identified component, automatically summarize its function by collecting tokens and behaviors whose probabilities most increase or decrease under calibrated interventions on the diagnostic set, clustering them, and prompting a judge model to produce concise hypotheses. Track the component dependency curves over generation steps to reveal when and how the capability is mediated. Package each component with: its effect size and CI, minimal counterfactual examples demonstrating knockout–rescue, the learned gate value if edited, and a natural-language description tied to its downstream impacts.

### Rationale
Causality requires interventions and counterfactuals, not correlations. By calibrating per-component interventions to a fixed KL distance, we obtain fair, comparable effect sizes across heterogeneous units and avoid over-penalizing sensitive layers. The active context search focuses measurement on cases where the capability is engaged, increasing statistical power and reducing spurious associations. Knockout–rescue tests and small-set ablations distinguish direct mediators from passengers and reveal redundancy, while robust aggregation prevents a few outlier prompts from dominating conclusions.

For editing, KL-constrained, trust-region gating provides local, controllable changes to the identified causal pathways while guarding against off-target drift. Output-based summaries convert raw effect measurements into human-usable interpretations, grounding component descriptions in their demonstrable downstream consequences rather than input correlations. Together, these steps reliably identify which components causally implement a capability and turn that knowledge into targeted, safe edits and clear interpretations.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [LLM_NLP] Automatically Interpreting Millions of Features in Large Language Models (2410.13928)
   problem: Some SAE features are better characterized by their causal effects on a model’s outputs rather than by correlational input contexts, which existing methods can miss; practitioners also need steerable, causally grounded interpretations.
   method:  They propose intervention scoring, which quantifies interpretability of a feature by the decrease in a scorer LLM’s surprisal about an interpretation when conditioned on generations from an intervened model. Intervention strength is calibrated per-feature via a target KL-divergence between clean and intervened logits, enabling fair comparison at fixed strengths. They automatically generate ‘output’ interpretations by showing the explainer only the tokens whose logits increase most after intervention, and validate that SAE features score higher than random SAEs or shuffled interpretations. Results show later layers have more interpretable output effects and that features with low correlational scores can be well explained through their downstream impacts.

2. [ROBOTICS] Search-Based Task Planning with Learned Skill Effect Models for Lifelong Robotic Manipulation (2109.08771)
   problem: Learning accurate, planning-relevant models of skill effects is hard because the distribution of states visited during planning/execution is unknown a priori, and the robot must continually incorporate new skills in a lifelong setting.
   method:  Interleave SEM training with planner-driven data collection. Initialize each new skill’s SEM with single-skill executions sampled from the known initial state distribution, then run the planner with current SEMs on training tasks to generate candidate paths. Sample paths from the planning graph—biased toward longer paths and those that include newly added skills—simulate them to collect (state, parameter, terminal state, cost) transitions, filter duplicates, and fine-tune SEMs for both new and existing skills as needed. This yields data concentrated on planning-relevant regions and supports incremental addition of skills; empirically, training on planner-generated data achieves higher success with fewer samples than training on random executions.

3. [ROBOTICS] Visual Geometric Skill Inference by Watching Human Demonstration (1911.04418)
   problem: Multiple associations can define the same skill and some may be occluded or leave the FOV, so the model must robustly and consistently select valid associations from only one demonstration.
   method:  Optimize each kernel with Incremental Maximum Entropy IRL over the demonstration frames. Define a reward r_t based on the decrease in overall control error between successive frames and assume a variational expert with confidence α (truncated-normal/Boltzmann selection over actions). Maximize demonstration likelihood by estimating the partition function via Monte Carlo and backpropagating through g_k and the GNN to update kernel parameters. Add a residual sum of weights (RSW) regularizer to make the select-out sparse/deterministic over the top-p alternatives while preserving the ability to switch to viable candidates when others are unobservable, yielding robust association inference.

4. [MULTIMODAL] VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation (1708.04686)
   problem: Existing VQA systems lack explicit supervision that links questions/answers to the precise image regions that constitute visual evidence, leading to weak or misaligned attentions; related annotations like human attention maps are coarse and bounding boxes often do not directly answer diverse question types.
   method:  Introduce VQS, a dataset that explicitly links COCO instance segmentations to VQA question–answer pairs by asking annotators to select the segmentation(s) that visually answer each question (with tight boxes when needed). This produces 37,868 images, 96,508 questions, 108,537 segmentations, and 43,725 boxes, enabling transfer of supervision between segmentation and VQA and supporting both supervised attention and new grounded tasks.

5. [MULTIMODAL] Multi-Modal Hallucination Control by Visual Information Grounding (2403.14003)
   problem: Autoregressive vision-language models (VLMs) often hallucinate objects or attributes because the model’s reliance on the visual prompt fades as more tokens are generated, and there is no principled, annotation-free way to quantify this grounding loss.
   method:  Introduce a visual Prompt Dependency Measure (PDM) that compares the conditioned distribution p(y_t | y_<t, x, c) to the unconditioned language-only distribution p(y_t | y_<t, x) using distances such as the Hellinger metric (PDM-H). This quantifies how much a token depends on the image versus the language prior. Empirically, PDM decreases with token position (conditioning dilution/fading memory), and lower PDM correlates with increased hallucinated objects in captions, while also identifying ‘contextual pressure’ cases (e.g., function words) where low PDM does not imply hallucination.

6. [MULTIMODAL] Words or Vision: Do Vision-Language Models Have Blind Faith in Text? (2503.02199)
   problem: Current VLM evaluations largely assume text inputs are consistent with images, leaving it unclear how models behave when visual and textual information conflict or when text is noisy, misleading, or irrelevant.
   method:  Construct a cross-task benchmark that injects three controlled textual variations—Match, Corruption, and Irrelevance—into four vision-centric datasets (VQAv2, DocVQA, MathVista, and a real-world Brand Recognition task). Define the Text Preference Ratio (TPR) to quantify whether models choose text- vs image-based answers when they disagree, alongside normalized accuracy to measure robustness. Evaluating 10 VLMs reveals a pervasive 'blind faith in text' (high TPR and large accuracy drops under corrupted text), especially in open models, highlighting safety risks in realistic settings such as brand recognition on webpages.

7. [CV] Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion (2012.02107)
   problem: Per-object, per-pixel feed-forward segmentations often conflict in overlap regions (multiple objects claiming the same pixels), especially under heavy or same-category occlusions, leading to erroneous masks and classifications.
   method:  Introduce an Occlusion Reasoning Module (ORM) that operates on the per-object likelihood maps. It detects conflicts where two objects both predict foreground, resolves them via pixel-level competition by assigning each contested pixel to the object with higher foreground likelihood, and then recovers the pairwise occlusion order by comparing the number of reassigned pixels in the conflict set. Using this order, it enforces consistent labeling in overlaps and feeds the corrected occlusion priors back into the CompositionalNet to mask occluded features and recompute class likelihoods, iterating this top-down self-correction to improve both segmentation and classification under occlusion.

8. [CV] Real-time Hyperspectral Imaging in Hardware via Trained Metasurface Encoders (2204.02084)
   problem: Fixed filter banks cannot optimally capture task-specific spectral features for downstream vision tasks like semantic segmentation, especially when RGB cues are ambiguous (e.g., real vs. artificial fruits with similar colors).
   method:  Develop a differentiable, physics-aware encoder–decoder framework (d-ALFRED) that jointly optimizes metasurface projector responses and geometry with the downstream decoder via backpropagation. The encoder is modeled by coupled-mode theory (with learnable resonance parameters mapped from geometry), enabling gradient flow through the metasurface transfer function; a U-Net decoder is trained end-to-end for pixel-wise classification. The learned spectral encoders yield compact barcodes that preserve discriminative cues, achieving mIoU ~0.81 (simulation) and ~0.74 (fabricated devices), outperforming an RGB-only baseline (~0.68 mIoU) and significantly improving IoU/F1 on challenging real–artificial fruit pairs.

9. [CV] PrPSeg: Universal Proposition Learning for Panoramic Renal Pathology Segmentation (2402.19286)
   problem: With partially labeled images, it is hard to enforce spatial correlations between labeled and unlabeled classes during training, limiting comprehensive multi-class segmentation.
   method:  Define an anatomy loss that operationalizes the universal proposition matrix for online semi-supervised learning: for a labeled class i, predictions for another class j on the same image are constrained so that (i) if i ⊃ j, j’s prediction stays within i; (ii) if i ⊂ j, j’s prediction covers i; and (iii) if i and j are exclusive, their overlap is minimized. This anatomy loss is combined with supervised BCE and Dice losses (weighted by λupl) to inject inter-object relational constraints and improve comprehensive segmentation.

10. [RL] Conformal Constrained Policy Optimization for Cost-Effective LLM Agents (2511.11828)
   problem: Directly learning set-valued (conformal) policies is combinatorial, and the distribution shift between behavior and target policies complicates reliable coverage calibration in sequential settings.
   method:  Introduce CCPO, which parameterizes a score policy π(a|o) and a threshold κ, and defines a stochastic surrogate S_{π,κ} that places uniform probability over the conformal action set C_{π,κ}(o). Train π with an actor–critic using V-trace off-policy corrections from π to S_{π,κ} with clipped importance weights, and perform a trust-region CPO update that optimizes advantages under a KL constraint while enforcing an upper-bounded coverage constraint; replace the non-differentiable indicator in C with a sigmoid softmask for stable gradients. Update κ online via decaying-step-size conformal prediction to track the target 1−α coverage, followed by a final batch calibration step; under mild assumptions this yields asymptotic 1−α coverage (and O(T^{-1/2+ξ}) coverage regret in adversarial streams) while minimizing cost.

11. [RL] Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL (2406.01939)
   problem: In supply chain RL (and many long-horizon MDPs), simulating a single trajectory under a state-dependent policy is a bottleneck because policy evaluations are inherently serial and expensive (often DNN-based), while standard parallel discrete event methods like Time Warp suffer rollbacks and GPU-unfriendly message passing.
   method:  Introduce Picard iteration for policy simulation: split the horizon [T] across M (virtual) processes and initialize a cache of actions for all time-steps. In each iteration, every process sweeps the entire trajectory but evaluates the policy π only on its assigned time-steps, using cached actions elsewhere (or aϕ if infeasible), while applying the cheap system transition f at every step; at iteration end, each process writes its evaluated actions back to the cache. This enables batched, GPU-friendly evaluation of π with no message passing and provably converges to the sequential trajectory in at most T iterations (Algorithm 1; correctness Proposition 2.1; batching and speedup discussion).

12. [MULTIMODAL] BabyWalk: Going Farther in Vision-and-Language Navigation by Taking Baby Steps (2005.04625)
   problem: Direct reinforcement learning on long-horizon VLN instructions is unstable, and imitation learning on whole trajectories does not adequately prepare agents for long, uncertain executions.
   method:  Use a two-phase training pipeline: (1) micro-level imitation learning where each BABY-STEP is learned independently with the agent 'preset' in expert context to match expert sub-trajectories; (2) curriculum-based reinforcement learning that progressively increases the number of consecutive BABY-STEPs the agent must execute (lectures), providing expert history up to the start and optimizing a fidelity-oriented reward (CLS) via policy gradients. This curriculum (up to 4 lectures) consistently improves over direct RL on full instructions, with stepwise gains in SDTW and CLS.

13. [IR_REC] FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding (2406.15657)
   problem: Inference-time relevance feedback for retrievers typically relies on cross-encoder scores and KL divergence, but listwise LLM rerankers output only orderings, making standard distillation inapplicable and potentially missing stronger supervision signals.
   method:  Use the LLM reranker’s ranked ordering as the supervision signal for updating retriever query embeddings, optimizing with the weighted RankNet loss over retrieved candidates to convert orderings into a differentiable objective. This enables relevance feedback without numeric scores and leverages the stronger listwise signal. On BEIR, LLM-based feedback improves recall@100 over cross-encoder feedback (e.g., 71.2% vs 69.0% average), and combining both signals yields further gains (72.0%).

14. [IR_REC] Intervention Harvesting for Context-Dependent Examination-Bias Estimation (1811.01802)
   problem: Unbiased learning-to-rank from clicks requires accurate examination-bias (propensity) estimates, but existing methods are restricted to the Position-Based Model (PBM) that ignores query/user context and often rely on intrusive randomized swaps or difficult document-level relevance modeling.
   method:  Introduce the Contextual Position-Based Model (CPBM), which models examination as Pr(E=1 | k, x) depending on rank k and context x, and learn it via a neural network h(k, x). Develop an AllPairs estimator that harvests implicit interventions from logs of at least two historic rankers (e.g., A/B tests or stationary deployments): define interventional sets S_{k,k'} of (x, d) that appear at both positions under different rankers, compute assignment weights q_k(x, d), form IPS-weighted click labels, and jointly fit h and an average-relevance model g(k, k', x) by maximizing a weighted cross-entropy objective. This controls for unobserved relevance without explicit swaps and yields context-specific propensities.

15. [IR_REC] Hybrid Inverted Index Is a Robust Accelerator for Dense Retrieval (2210.05521)
   problem: Selecting salient query terms can add undesirable online cost if it relies on heavy neural processing, yet accurate lexical cues are needed to recover relevant documents missed by clustering.
   method:  HI2 makes query-time term selection lightweight by performing all heavy term scoring offline on documents (BM25 or BERT+MLP), storing per-term scores and the global average score per term. At search time, it selects all tokens for short queries and only the top-K_T2 tokens by average score for long queries, avoiding neural computation on queries. This keeps query dispatch overhead negligible while preserving the lexical benefits that improve candidate recall and overall efficiency.

16. [SPEECH] DNCASR: End-to-End Training for Speaker-Attributed ASR (2506.01916)
   problem: Speaker-attributed ASR for long multi-party meetings needs consistent meeting-wide speaker indices and accurate attribution in overlapping speech, but prior integrated or parallel systems either require non-neural clustering or suffer from misalignment between ASR outputs and speaker index prediction.
   method:  Propose DNCASR, an end-to-end trainable architecture with two encoders (a waveform encoder for local token-level acoustic/lexical features and a speaker encoder for window-level speaker embeddings) and two linked decoders (an SOT ASR decoder and a neural clustering (DNC) decoder). A novel cross-attention link allows the DNC decoder to attend directly to the ASR decoder’s token-level hidden features (W_CA), aligning predicted speaker indices with the serialized word tokens and enabling joint optimization under a single loss. Compared to a parallel DNC+ASR baseline, DNCASR yields lower speaker-attributed error (e.g., AMI-MDM Eval cpWER 31.5% with CDA vs 34.6% parallel; 9.0% relative reduction) and larger gains on multi-talker segments.

17. [SPEECH] Unlocking Speech Instruction Data Potential with Query Rewriting (2507.08603)
   problem: Validating that TTS-synthesized speech preserves the linguistic content of the source text is unreliable with a single ASR or WER-only checks; ASR errors lead to improper filtering and dataset bias.
   method:  Use multi-agent annotation and verification: transcribe each synthesized audio with three diverse ASR models (whisper-large-v3, canary-1b, parakeet-tdt-1.1b) and compute semantic similarity to the original text using three embedding models (gte-large-en-v1.5, mxbai-embed-large-v1, stella_en_400M_v5). Average across ASRs and embeddings to form a SIM score and filter with a threshold (α=0.9). This ensemble reduces annotation errors and aligns well with WER (e.g., >98% consistency when WER=0) and improves WER over single-ASR selection (Table 4).

18. [SPEECH] RED-ACE: Robust Error Detection for ASR using Confidence Embeddings (2203.07172)
   problem: ASR error detection models typically rely only on the transcription text and ignore auxiliary ASR signals like word-level confidences, limiting their ability to accurately flag recognition errors.
   method:  Introduce RED-ACE, a BERT-based sequence tagger augmented with a confidence embedding layer. ASR word-level confidence scores are quantized into bins and mapped via a learned embedding matrix; the confidence embedding is summed with the token, segment, and position embeddings to form each token’s input. This joint, contextualized encoding of text and confidence yields statistically significant F1 gains over text-only BERT and over naive score fusion (e.g., appending raw scores, BERT C) on LibriSpeech clean/other (e.g., +4.9% and +7.4% F1), remains robust under cross-pool shifts (+6.3% and +9.9% F1), and generalizes across different ASR models (+2.6% and +3.8% F1).

19. [ROBOTICS] Statler: State-Maintaining Language Models for Embodied Reasoning (2306.17840)
   problem: LLM-based robot planners (e.g., Code-as-Policies) condition only on action/observation histories and struggle with long-horizon temporal reasoning and latent/unobservable dynamics, leading to brittle behavior on multi-step tasks.
   method:  Statler introduces explicit world-state maintenance with two prompted LLMs. A world-state reader takes the user query plus the current JSON-formatted state and generates executable code for the robot; when state changes are implicated, the code calls update_wm to invoke a world-state writer. The writer updates the state estimate based on the action/query, and the updated state is stored in external memory and conditions future decisions. This model-based extension of CaP simplifies tracking and consulting state at each step and yields large gains on tabletop manipulation tasks and a real UR5 setup, especially on temporal queries (e.g., 83% vs. 31% on Pick-and-Place temporal queries; higher episode success across domains).

20. [ROBOTICS] Kinematic-aware Prompting for Generalizable Articulated Object Manipulation with LLMs (2311.02847)
   problem: LLM-based robotic systems struggle to manipulate diverse articulated objects because they lack an explicit, uniform understanding of object kinematics (parts, joints, and contact points), especially in real-world settings.
   method:  Introduce a Unified Kinematic Knowledge Parser that converts each object's geometric-linked parts, joint types/properties, and contact locations into a structured textual (.xml-like) description that LLMs can reliably parse. In simulation this info is extracted directly; in the real world it is obtained via a perception pipeline (Grounding-DINO + Segment Anything for segmentation and GAPartNet for actionable parts and joint estimation). This unified, code-like description supplies LLMs with the precise kinematic context needed for manipulation policy generation.

21. [ROBOTICS] Robust Instant Policy: Leveraging Student's t-Regression Model for Robust In-context Imitation Learning of Robot Manipulation (2506.15157)
   problem: LLM-based in-context imitation learning for robot manipulation can hallucinate, producing outlier trajectories that deviate from few-shot demonstrations, leading to failures in continuous control.
   method:  Robust Instant Policy (RIP) queries an LLM instant policy (via KAT) multiple times on the same context to sample Q candidate trajectories, then fits a Student's t-regression model Sθ(a_t|t;ν) with neural networks for mean and variance to the sampled actions over time, leveraging heavy-tailed robustness to outliers. The final plan is the time-wise mean of the fitted t-distribution, effectively ignoring hallucinated trajectories. Ablations show ν=1.5 outperforms Gaussian (ν→∞), and RIP improves success rates by 26% over KAT and 16% over a Gaussian aggregator across 10 manipulation tasks, especially in low-data regimes.

## Tie-breaking
When between two adjacent scores, default to the LOWER. Resist
optimism bias toward "novel". Score against the literature
signal only; do not apply an additional penalty when the method
reuses inspired seeds (the seeds are context, not a benchmark).

   Mechanistic interpretability seeks to localize model behavior to the internal components that causally realize it. Prior work has advanced activation-space localization and causal tracing, but modules that appear important in activation space may merely aggregate or amplify upstream signals rather than encode the target capability in their own parameters. To address this gap, we propose Weight Patching, a parameter-space intervention method for source-oriented analysis in paired same-architecture models that differ in how strongly they express a target capability under the inputs of interest. Given a base model and a behavior-specialized counterpart, Weight Patching replaces selected module weights from the specialized model into the base model under a fixed input. We instantiate the method on instruction following and introduce a framework centered on a vector-anchor behavioral interface that provides a shared internal criterion for whether a task-relevant control state has been formed or recovered in open-ended generation. Under this framework, the analysis reveals a hierarchy from shallow candidate source-side carriers to aggregation and routing modules, and further to downstream execution circuits. The recovered component scores can also guide mechanism-aware model merging, improving selective fusion across the evaluated expert combinations and providing additional external validation.

1. Locating and Mitigating Gender Bias in Large Language Models (International Conference on Intelligent Computing 2024, cites: 12)
   Large language models(LLM) are pre-trained on extensive corpora to learn facts and human cognition which contain human preferences. However, this process can inadvertently lead to these models acquiring biases and stereotypes prevalent in society. Prior research has typically tackled the issue of bias through a one-dimensional perspective, concentrating either on locating or mitigating it. This limited perspective has created obstacles in facilitating research on bias to synergistically complement and progressively build upon one another. In this study, we integrate the processes of locating and mitigating bias within a unified framework. Initially, we use causal mediation analysis to trace the causal effects of different components' activation within a large language model. Building on this, we propose the LSDM (Least Square Debias Method), a knowledge-editing based method for mitigating gender bias in occupational pronouns, and compare it against two baselines on three gender bias datasets and seven knowledge competency test datasets. The experimental results indicate that the primary contributors to gender bias are the bottom MLP modules acting on the last token of occupational pronouns and the top attention module acting on the final word in the sentence. Furthermore, LSDM mitigates gender bias in the model more effectively than the other baselines, while fully preserving the model's capabilities in all other aspects.

2. Do LLMs "Feel"? Emotion Circuits Discovery and Control (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 7)
   As the demand for emotional intelligence in large language models (LLMs) grows, a key challenge lies in understanding the internal mechanisms that give rise to emotional expression and in controlling emotions in generated text. This study addresses three core questions: (1) Do LLMs contain context-agnostic mechanisms shaping emotional expression? (2) What form do these mechanisms take? (3) Can they be harnessed for universal emotion control? We first construct a controlled dataset, SEV (Scenario-Event with Valence), to elicit comparable internal states across emotions. Subsequently, we extract context-agnostic emotion directions that reveal consistent, cross-context encoding of emotion (Q1). We identify neurons and attention heads that locally implement emotional computation through analytical decomposition and causal analysis, and validate their causal roles via ablation and enhancement interventions. Next, we quantify each sublayer's causal influence on the model's final emotion representation and integrate the identified local components into coherent global emotion circuits that drive emotional expression (Q2). Directly modulating these circuits achieves 99.65% emotion-expression accuracy on the test set, surpassing prompting- and steering-based methods (Q3). To our knowledge, this is the first systematic study to uncover and validate emotion circuits in LLMs, offering new insights into interpretability and controllable emotional intelligence.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
